ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
for r in range(15, 24):

</parameter>
</function>

Now I see the characters have moved even closer. The black character is now at cols 24-32 and the dark gray at cols 33-38. But wait, the dark gray character only has 18 pixels now (down from 45). It seems to be getting partially obscured or clipped by the divider.

- Row 15: `bbbbBBBBBBBBBccccccbbbbbb` - black character (B) and dark gray (c) are now adjacent!
- The black character extends to col 32 (the right edge of the divider)
- The dark gray character starts at col 33
But the dark gray character only has 18 pixels now vs 45 before. It seems like the character is being "eaten" or clipped as they get closer. 
Wait, looking more carefully at the segmentation:
- id=5 (B, black): bbox [15,24]-[23,32] - this is the black character, now touching the divider
- id=6 (c, dark gray): bbox [15,33]-[17,38] with only 18 pixels - this is now much smaller
The dark gray character was originally an L-shape with 45 pixels. Now it's only 18 pixels and only spans rows 15-17 (was 15-23). So the bottom part of the L has been "eaten" or covered.
Looking at the ASCII again:
Row 15: `bbbbBBBBBBBBBccccccbbbbbb` - both characters visible on same row
Row 16: `bbbbBWBBWBBSBccccccbbbbbb`
Row 17: `bbbbBBBBBBBBBccccccbbbbbb`
Row 18: `bbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbb` - dark gray gone below row 17
So the dark gray character is being clipped as the black character moves into its space. The black character seems to be "eating" or "absorbing" the dark gray one.
The goal might be to completely absorb the dark gray character by moving RIGHT until it's fully consumed. Let me try more RIGHT actions.
But wait - I should also check if the yellow L-shape at the bottom is relevant. Let me also note that the divider (light blue, S) seems to be shrinking - it was 189 pixels, now it's split into two parts: 45 pixels (top) and 117 pixels (bottom).
Let me try RIGHT a few more times to see if the dark gray character gets fully consumed.
